question:
Let $f$ be a polynomial with positive integer coefficients. Prove
that if $n$ is a positive integer, then $f(n)$ divides $f(f(n)+1)$ if
and only if $n=1$. [Editor's note: one must assume $f$ is nonconstant.]
solution:
The problem fails if $f$ is allowed to be constant, e.g., take $f(n) = 1$.
We thus assume that $f$ is nonconstant.
Write $f(n) = \sum_{i=0}^d a_i n^i$ with $a_i > 0$. Then
\begin{align*}
f(f(n)+1) &= \sum_{i=0}^d a_i (f(n) + 1)^i \\
&\equiv f(1) \pmod{f(n)}.
\end{align*}
If $n = 1$, then this implies that $f(f(n)+1)$ is divisible by $f(n)$.
Otherwise, $0 < f(1) < f(n)$ since $f$ is nonconstant and has positive
coefficients, so $f(f(n)+1)$ cannot be divisible by $f(n)$.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $polyfun$ be a polynomial with positive integer coefficients. Prove
that if $posintn$ is a positive integer, then $polyfun(posintn)$ divides $polyfun(polyfun(posintn)+1)$ if
and only if $posintn=1$. [Editor's note: one must assume $polyfun$ is nonconstant.]
descriptive_long.solution:
The problem fails if $polyfun$ is allowed to be constant, e.g., take $polyfun(posintn) = 1$.
We thus assume that $polyfun$ is nonconstant.
Write $polyfun(posintn) = \sum_{sumidxi=0}^{degrees} coeffi \, posintn^{sumidxi}$ with $coeffi > 0$. Then
\begin{align*}
polyfun(polyfun(posintn)+1) &= \sum_{sumidxi=0}^{degrees} coeffi \, (polyfun(posintn) + 1)^{sumidxi} \\
&\equiv polyfun(1) \pmod{polyfun(posintn)}.
\end{align*}
If $posintn = 1$, then this implies that $polyfun(polyfun(posintn)+1)$ is divisible by $polyfun(posintn)$.
Otherwise, $0 < polyfun(1) < polyfun(posintn)$ since $polyfun$ is nonconstant and has positive
coefficients, so $polyfun(polyfun(posintn)+1)$ cannot be divisible by $polyfun(posintn)$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $pineapple$ be a polynomial with positive integer coefficients. Prove
that if $rhinocero$ is a positive integer, then $pineapple(rhinocero)$ divides $pineapple(pineapple(rhinocero)+1)$ if
and only if $rhinocero=1$. [Editor's note: one must assume $pineapple$ is nonconstant.]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The problem fails if $pineapple$ is allowed to be constant, e.g., take $pineapple(rhinocero) = 1$.
We thus assume that $pineapple$ is nonconstant.
Write $pineapple(rhinocero) = \sum_{tangerine=0}^{saxophone} windsock_{tangerine} rhinocero^{tangerine}$ with $windsock_{tangerine} > 0$. Then
\begin{align*}
pineapple(pineapple(rhinocero)+1) &= \sum_{tangerine=0}^{saxophone} windsock_{tangerine} (pineapple(rhinocero) + 1)^{tangerine} \\
&\equiv pineapple(1) \pmod{pineapple(rhinocero)}.
\end{align*}
If $rhinocero = 1$, then this implies that $pineapple(pineapple(rhinocero)+1)$ is divisible by $pineapple(rhinocero)$.
Otherwise, $0 < pineapple(1) < pineapple(rhinocero)$ since $pineapple$ is nonconstant and has positive
coefficients, so $pineapple(pineapple(rhinocero)+1)$ cannot be divisible by $pineapple(rhinocero)$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Let $nonpolyfn$ be a polynomial with positive integer coefficients. Prove that if $irrational$ is a positive integer, then $nonpolyfn(irrational)$ divides $nonpolyfn(nonpolyfn(irrational)+1)$ if and only if $irrational=1$. [Editor's note: one must assume $nonpolyfn$ is nonconstant.]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The problem fails if $nonpolyfn$ is allowed to be constant, e.g., take $nonpolyfn(irrational) = 1$.
We thus assume that $nonpolyfn$ is nonconstant.
Write $nonpolyfn(irrational) = \sum_{constant=0}^{nodegree} variable \; irrational^{constant}$ with $variable > 0$. Then
\begin{align*}
nonpolyfn(nonpolyfn(irrational)+1) &= \sum_{constant=0}^{nodegree} variable \,(nonpolyfn(irrational) + 1)^{constant} \\
&\equiv nonpolyfn(1) \pmod{nonpolyfn(irrational)}.
\end{align*}
If $irrational = 1$, then this implies that $nonpolyfn(nonpolyfn(irrational)+1)$ is divisible by $nonpolyfn(irrational)$.
Otherwise, $0 < nonpolyfn(1) < nonpolyfn(irrational)$ since $nonpolyfn$ is nonconstant and has positive
coefficients, so $nonpolyfn(nonpolyfn(irrational)+1)$ cannot be divisible by $nonpolyfn(irrational)$.

garbled_string.question:
Let $mnbvcxql$ be a polynomial with positive integer coefficients. Prove
that if $qzxwvtnp$ is a positive integer, then $mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)$ divides $mnbvcxql(mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)+1)$ if
and only if $qzxwvtnp=1$. [Editor's note: one must assume $mnbvcxql$ is nonconstant.]
garbled_string.solution:
The problem fails if $mnbvcxql$ is allowed to be constant, e.g., take $mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp) = 1$.
We thus assume that $mnbvcxql$ is nonconstant.
Write $mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp) = \sum_{i=0}^{pqlmnvzb} hskdjfla\, qzxwvtnp^i$ with $hskdjfla > 0$. Then
\begin{align*}
mnbvcxql(mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)+1) &= \sum_{i=0}^{pqlmnvzb} hskdjfla\, (mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp) + 1)^i \\
&\equiv mnbvcxql(1) \pmod{mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)}.
\end{align*}
If $qzxwvtnp = 1$, then this implies that $mnbvcxql(mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)+1)$ is divisible by $mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)$.
Otherwise, $0 < mnbvcxql(1) < mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)$ since $mnbvcxql$ is nonconstant and has positive
coefficients, so $mnbvcxql(mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)+1)$ cannot be divisible by $mnbvcxql(qzxwvtnp)$.}  Strouvezgne\omega d

kernel_variant.question:
Let $f(x)=a_{0}+a_{1}x+ oldsymbol{ igl(	ext{non-negative integer coefficients} igr)}\cdots +a_{d}x^{d}$ be a non-constant polynomial whose constant term satisfies $a_{0}>0$ and whose highest-degree coefficient $a_{d}>0$.  Show that for a non-negative integer $n$,
\[
     f(n)\mid f\bigl(f(n)\bigr)\qquad\text{iff}\qquad n=0.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Write f(n)=\sum _{i=0}^d a_i n^i.  Because every a_i\geq 0 and at least one a_i with i\geq 1 is positive, we have
f(n)>a_0=f(0)  for every n\geq 1.  (1)

Step 1 - Work modulo f(n).  Expand at x=f(n):
f(f(n))=\sum _{i=0}^d a_i (f(n))^i.

Step 2 - Collapse the powers.  Since (f(n))^i\equiv 0 (mod f(n)) for every i\geq 1, the expansion reduces to
f(f(n))\equiv a_0=f(0)  (mod f(n)).  (2)

Step 3 - Translate divisibility.  Condition f(n)\mid f(f(n)) coupled with (2) gives
f(n)\mid f(0)=a_0.  (3)

Step 4 - Rule out n\geq 1.  By (1) we have f(n)>a_0 whenever n\geq 1, contradicting (3).  Hence no positive n works.

Step 5 - Verify n=0.  For n=0 we have f(0)=a_0>0 and
f(f(0))=f(a_0)=\sum _{i=0}^d a_i a_0^i,
which is a sum of terms each divisible by a_0.  Thus a_0=f(0)\mid f(f(0)), satisfying the condition.

Combining Steps 4 and 5, the divisibility holds exactly for n=0, completing the proof.

core_steps: Expand f(f(n)+1) and work modulo f(n)
Positive-coefficient monotonicity yields f(1) < f(n) for n>1, so only n=1 works

slot1.description: Fixed constant added to f(n) inside the outer evaluation (currently "+1"); replacing it by any integer c would change the residue to f(c) and the candidate n=c but leaves the argument structure intact.

slot2.description: Stipulation that every coefficient is strictly positive; relaxing this to “non-negative integer coefficients with at least one positive coefficient of positive degree” keeps the key inequality f(1) < f(n) for n>1 and thus preserves the proof flow.
